1: A type: “Hear this word that the LORD has spoken against you, O people of Israel — against the whole family I brought up from the land of Egypt.” The summons to “hear” (shimʿu) is the prophetic call to attention that the NT applies to Christ’s proclamation: “He who has ears, let him hear” (Matthew 11:15). The word against the whole family is simultaneously a claim of election and of covenant accountability — you are the family I saved, therefore you are responsible. The NT extension: Christ addresses the new covenant family — those brought up from the death of sin through his Exodus (Romans 6:3-4) — with the same combination of privilege and responsibility (1 Peter 1:17).
2: A type: “You only have I known of all the families of the earth; therefore I will punish you for all your iniquities.” The yādaʿ (know) here is the covenantal intimacy that founds accountability — the logic of greater privilege, greater responsibility. This verse is the OT foundation for Paul’s argument in Romans 2:9-10: “There will be tribulation and distress for every human being who does evil, the Jew first and also the Greek.” In Christ, the elect’s accountability is fully met — he bears the “punishment for all your iniquities” that the elect should receive, becoming the one who was fully known by God and fully punished on their behalf (2 Corinthians 5:21).
3: A shadow: “Can two walk together unless they have agreed?” The rhetorical questions of Amos 3:3-8 all argue from cause to effect — no effect without a corresponding cause. YHWH’s judgment is not arbitrary; it is the effect of Israel’s refusal to walk with him. The covenant-walk is the OT’s metaphor for ongoing relationship with God (Micah 6:8: “walk humbly with your God”). Christ is the one who walks with his disciples (Luke 24:15: “Jesus himself drew near and went with them”) and who makes agreement possible: “I am the way” (John 14:6) — the path on which the walk with God proceeds.
4: A shadow: “Does a lion roar in the forest when it has no prey?” The logic of cause-and-effect continues. YHWH’s roaring in Amos 3:1:2 is not empty noise — there is a prey (Israel’s sin). The lion-as-judge appears in the NT applied to Christ: the Lion of the tribe of Judah (Revelation 5:5) who is simultaneously the Lamb who was slain. The same figure judges and redeems — the roar of Amos’s lion is the sound of the same divine authority that, from the cross, declares “It is finished” (John 19:30).
5: A shadow: “Does a bird fall to the ground in a snare when no trap has been set?” The trap has been set — Israel’s covenant-violations are the snare of their own making. The NT uses snare-imagery for the unexpected arrival of judgment: “that day come upon you suddenly like a trap” (Luke 21:34-35). The call to watchfulness in the Gospels (Matthew 24:42-44) is the pastoral application of Amos’s logical sequence: if the trap is set, the bird will fall; if judgment is coming, be ready.
6: A shadow: “When a ram’s horn sounds the alarm in a city, do the people not tremble? When disaster strikes a city, has not the LORD done it?” The rhetorical question about YHWH’s sovereignty over disaster is the OT’s strong providentialism. The NT refines but does not abandon this: “Are not two sparrows sold for a penny? And not one of them will fall to the ground apart from your Father” (Matthew 10:29). Christ is both the one through whom the Father’s sovereignty operates and the one who faced the ultimate “disaster” — the crucifixion — in which God’s judgment and mercy met.
7: A fulfillment: “For the Lord GOD does nothing without first disclosing his plans to his servants the prophets.” This is the OT’s strongest statement of prophetic disclosure. Jesus takes up the same principle and claims its ultimate expression: “No longer do I call you servants, for the servant does not know what his master is doing; but I have called you friends, for all that I have heard from my Father I have made known to you” (John 15:15). The prophet-disclosure pattern of Amos 3:7 is fulfilled not merely in the writing prophets but in Christ, who reveals the Father fully (John 1:18: “the only God, who is at the Father’s side, he has made him known”). Revelation 1:1 explicitly echoes Amos 3:7’s structure: God reveals to his servant.
8: A type: “The lion has roared — who will not be afraid? The Lord GOD has spoken — who can withhold prophecy?” The compulsion of the prophetic word is the basis of prophetic authority — it cannot be suppressed. The apostles embody this: “We cannot but speak of what we have seen and heard” (Acts 4:20). The word of God that compels prophecy is ultimately the Word made flesh — Christ himself is the speaking of God that cannot be silenced even by death. The resurrection is YHWH’s roar that demands response.
9: A shadow: Ashdod and Egypt are summoned to witness Israel’s disorder and oppression — the pagan nations are called as witnesses against the covenant people. The irony: Israel, called to be a witness to the nations (Isaiah 43:10), is instead witnessed against by the nations. Christ reverses this: the church is now the witness to the nations (Acts 1:8: “you will be my witnesses... to the end of the earth”), testifying to the risen Lord rather than manifesting the disorder that Amos exposes.
10: A shadow: “They do not know how to act with integrity — those who hoard violence and plunder in their strongholds.” The not-knowing of Israel is a moral blindness, not merely an intellectual gap. Jesus applies the same diagnosis: “Father, forgive them, for they do not know what they are doing” (Luke 23:34) — the not-knowing of the crucifiers parallels Israel’s moral blindness. The remedy for both is not information but transformation: “the knowledge of the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ” (2 Corinthians 4:6).
11: A shadow: an enemy will surround the land, strip away the strongholds, and plunder the fortresses. The stripping of the strongholds that housed stolen wealth is the reversal of the hoarding condemned in Amos 3:10. The pattern runs into the NT: the rich man’s storehouses are torn down (Luke 12:18-20); the city built on plunder falls (Revelation 18:11-13). Christ’s own poverty — “though he was rich, yet for your sake he became poor” (2 Corinthians 8:9) — is the counterpoint to the strongholds that Israel filled with violence.
12: A type: as the shepherd rescues from the lion’s mouth only two legs or a scrap of an ear, so a remnant of Israel will be saved. The remnant-typology is the OT’s consistent pattern: not all Israel will perish; a small, humble remnant survives the lion’s mouth. Paul applies this precisely: “So too at the present time there is a remnant, chosen by grace” (Romans 11:5). Christ is the good shepherd who loses none that the Father gives him (John 6:39) — the remnant preserved from the lion is the flock he carries on his shoulders (Luke 15:5).
13: A shadow: “Hear this and testify against the house of Jacob, declares the Lord GOD, the God of armies.” The summons to testimony against Jacob is the covenant lawsuit (rîb) pattern. The NT uses courtroom language for Christ’s relation to Israel: “Do not think that I will accuse you to the Father. There is one who accuses you: Moses, on whom you have set your hope” (John 5:45). The accuser of Israel is the Law they profess to follow; Christ is simultaneously advocate (1 John 2:1) and judge (John 5:22).
14: A fulfillment-anticipation: “On the day I call Israel to account for its crimes, I will also strike down the altars of Bethel.” The false sanctuary at Bethel — set up by Jeroboam I as a rival to Jerusalem — falls under judgment alongside Israel’s ethical crimes. Jesus’s cleansing of the Jerusalem temple (John 2:13-22) and his prediction of its destruction (“not one stone will be left on another,” Matthew 24:2) are the fulfillment of this pattern: the sanctuary that Israel has turned into an instrument of religious comfort for the comfortable will fall. Christ himself becomes the new temple (John 2:21).
15: A shadow: winter and summer houses demolished; houses adorned with ivory perish; great houses end. The luxury of Israel’s ruling class — ivory-decorated houses that required the exploitation Amos condemns — will be stripped away. The NT’s parable of Dives and Lazarus (Luke 16:19-31) is the same pattern compressed: the purple-clad rich man loses everything; the poor man is carried to Abraham’s side. Christ’s warning — “woe to you who are rich, for you have received your consolation” (Luke 6:24) — is Amos 3:15 applied to his own generation.
